title: Daily Briefing — Monday, June 1
tldr: MiniMax M3 launched — an open-weight model claiming SWE-Bench Pro 59 (ahead of GPT-5.5 & Gemini 3.1 Pro), a 1M-token context, and native multimodality, at ~5–10% of frontier pricing. A second Chinese lab in frontier-class tier.
Anthropic confidentially filed its S-1 (IPO path) at a reported ~$47B revenue run-rate — ~5× year-over-year.
GitHub Copilot's token-based billing kicked in June 1, drawing developer backlash over cost increases.

blocks.sub: MiniMax M3: open-weight, 1M-context, frontier-class — at a fraction of the price
blocks.p: MiniMax (Shanghai) launched M3 on Jun 1 (weights + technical report to follow within ~10 days — treat benchmarks as directional until independent reproduction). Claims: SWE-Bench Pro 59 (ahead of GPT-5.5 and Gemini 3.1 Pro), 1M-token context (5× M2.7), native image/video understanding, and OpenRouter pricing around 5–10% of frontier proprietary tokens. The novelty is MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA) — ~15.6× faster decode and ~9.7× faster prefill at 1M context vs M2.
blocks.why: Saturday's price-spread thread (DeepSeek 100× cheaper output) just got a second Chinese open-weight entrant in frontier-class tier — it's now a tier, not a model. And 1M context as a commodity erodes a lot of RAG complexity: if your retrieval pipeline mainly exists to compress context, sparse-attention long-context models are the architectural shift that eats it.
blocks.doIt: When M3 weights ship (~Jun 11), run your hardest long-context tasks against M3 vs DeepSeek V4-Pro vs Opus 4.8: a ~500K-token monorepo refactor, a 200-page document synthesis, a 50-file PR review. Pick the winner per task class, not per app.

blocks.p: Anthropic confidentially filed a draft S-1 with the SEC (Jun 1) after the $65B Series H, on a reported ~$47B revenue run-rate (up from ~$10B a year ago — fast-moving, directional).
blocks.why: This is the first major frontier-lab IPO path. Once it prices, Anthropic's compute spend and gross margins become public — permanently changing how the whole industry is benchmarked.
blocks.doIt: Watch for the eventual public financials: they'll be the first real, audited look at frontier-lab unit economics — gold for anyone modeling AI build-vs-buy costs.

blocks.p: Copilot's shift to token-based “AI Credits” took effect Jun 1, with developers reporting sharp cost increases versus the old flat fee.
blocks.why: Continues last week's cost-reckoning thread: unmetered agentic coding is being repriced toward usage. The winners measure cost-per-task, not seats.
blocks.doIt: Instrument per-workflow AI spend now, and route cheap/high-volume tasks to a Flash-class or open-weight model so a billing change doesn't blindside your budget.

blocks.sub: Treat unverified benchmark claims as hypotheses, not facts
blocks.p: M3's SWE-Bench Pro 59 is a vendor claim before the weights and report are out. The disciplined move: log such claims as hypotheses with a verification date (here, ~Jun 11 when weights land), then confirm against your own eval before changing any default. This habit — never repricing your stack on a pre-release number — is what separates teams that ride the cheap-model wave from teams that get burned by a benchmark that didn't reproduce.

sources: DataNorth / WinBuzzer / MLQ.ai / TechTimes / Codersera (MiniMax M3; SWE-Bench Pro 59 claim; 1M context; MSA) · The Neuron (Jun 1 recap) · CNBC / TechCrunch (Anthropic confidential S-1; ~$47B run-rate) · buildfastwithai (Jun 1 roundup; Copilot token billing). Benchmark and run-rate figures directional until independently verified.
